scenario_blurb: Stage IIIB-IV non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) with EGFR activating mutations (L858R or exon 19 deletion). Patients treated with EGFR TKIs (erlotinib, osimertinib) with eventual T790M resistance. Include NGS testing, brain metastases in some patients.

events.text: Patient is a 56-year-old Middle Eastern male, never smoker. ECOG performance status 0. No significant past medical history. Incidental finding on pre-employment chest X-ray.

Diagnosed with Stage IIIB non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC), adenocarcinoma histology (ICD-O code 8140/3), primary site left lower lobe (C34.3). TNM staging T3N2M0 with ipsilateral mediastinal lymphadenopathy and chest wall invasion. No distant metastases.

CT-guided biopsy of left lower lobe mass reveals moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma with acinar predominant pattern and focal signet ring features. TTF-1 positive, Napsin A positive, CK7 positive. Specimen submitted for comprehensive genomic profiling.

NGS (Tempus xT) on left lower lobe biopsy. EGFR exon 19 deletion (p.L747_T751del) detected at VAF 44%. TP53 G266V mutation (VAF 38%). APC truncating mutation (VAF 15%). No ALK, ROS1, BRAF, MET, RET, HER2, or KRAS alterations. PD-L1 TPS 5%. TMB 3 mutations/Mb.

Assessment: Stage IIIB unresectable EGFR exon 19 deletion-positive adenocarcinoma with chest wall invasion and N2 disease. Plan: Concurrent chemoradiation per standard of care followed by consolidation osimertinib. Staging brain MRI.

CT shows 4.5 cm left lower lobe mass abutting and invading the posterolateral chest wall with rib erosion. Ipsilateral mediastinal lymphadenopathy at stations 5 and 7 (largest 2.3 cm). No pleural effusion.
PET/CT confirms FDG-avid left lower lobe mass (SUV max 11.8) and FDG-avid mediastinal nodes (SUV max 9.2). No distant FDG-avid disease.
MRI brain negative for intracranial metastases.
Started concurrent carboplatin AUC 2 weekly with thoracic radiation.
Thoracic radiation therapy to left lower lobe primary with chest wall involvement and involved mediastinal lymph node stations, 60 Gy in 30 fractions.
Grade 2 radiation pneumonitis developing 4 weeks post-completion of radiation. Treated with prednisone 40 mg with slow taper over 8 weeks.

Post-chemoradiation CT shows left lower lobe mass decreased from 4.5 cm to 2.2 cm. Mediastinal lymphadenopathy resolved. Patchy ground-glass opacities in left lower lobe consistent with radiation pneumonitis. No distant disease.

Assessment: Good response to chemoradiation. Radiation pneumonitis improving on steroid taper. Plan: Start consolidation osimertinib once steroid dose is below prednisone 10 mg daily to avoid confounding immune suppression. Monitor for pneumonitis flare.
Started consolidation osimertinib 80 mg daily after resolution of radiation pneumonitis and completion of steroid taper.

Mild recurrence of grade 1 pneumonitis-like symptoms 2 weeks after starting osimertinib. CT shows stable ground-glass opacities. Managed with short course of prednisone 20 mg taper. Osimertinib continued.
CT at 3 months on consolidation osimertinib shows left lower lobe residual 1.8 cm with fibrotic changes. Pneumonitis opacities resolved. No distant disease.
CT at 6 months shows left lower lobe 1.6 cm. Post-radiation fibrosis. No recurrence.
CT at 9 months stable. Left lower lobe residual 1.5 cm. No progression.
Assessment: Stable disease at 9 months on consolidation osimertinib. Pneumonitis resolved. Tolerating treatment well. Plan: Continue osimertinib. Surveillance imaging every 3 months.
CT at 12 months stable. Left lower lobe 1.5 cm with fibrosis. No new lesions.

documents.text:
SURGICAL PATHOLOGY REPORT

SPECIMEN ID: SP-25-04821

TYPE OF PROCEDURE: CT-guided percutaneous core needle biopsy

SPECIMEN TYPE: Left lower lobe lung mass, core biopsy (4 cores)

CLINICAL HISTORY: 56-year-old male, never smoker, with incidentally discovered left lower lobe mass on pre-employment chest X-ray. CT demonstrates 4.5 cm mass with chest wall invasion and ipsilateral mediastinal lymphadenopathy. Biopsy for tissue diagnosis.

GROSS PATHOLOGY:
Received in formalin labeled "left lower lobe lung mass" are four tan-white cylindrical core biopsy fragments measuring 1.0 to 1.5 cm in length and 0.1 cm in diameter. The tissue is firm and tan-white. Entirely submitted in one cassette.

DIAGNOSTIC FINDINGS:
Sections show cores of lung parenchyma extensively infiltrated by a moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma. The tumor exhibits an acinar predominant growth pattern with glandular structures lined by columnar cells demonstrating moderate nuclear atypia, vesicular chromatin, and occasional prominent nucleoli. Focal areas show signet ring cell morphology comprising approximately 10% of the tumor. Desmoplastic stromal reaction is present. Mitotic figures are identified (approximately 8 per 10 high-power fields). No definite lymphovascular invasion is identified in the cores sampled. Necrosis is not prominent.

ANCILLARY STUDIES:
Immunohistochemistry (performed on block A1):
  TTF-1: Positive (strong, diffuse nuclear staining)
  Napsin A: Positive (granular cytoplasmic staining)
  CK7: Positive (diffuse membranous staining)
  CK20: Negative
  p40: Negative
  Synaptophysin: Negative
  Chromogranin: Negative
  Ki-67: Approximately 25% proliferative index

The immunoprofile is consistent with primary pulmonary adenocarcinoma. The combination of TTF-1 and Napsin A positivity with CK7 positivity and CK20 negativity supports a lung primary.

FINAL DIAGNOSIS:
Left lower lobe lung, CT-guided core biopsy:
- Moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma, acinar predominant pattern with focal signet ring cell features
- Immunophenotype consistent with primary pulmonary adenocarcinoma
- Tissue submitted for comprehensive genomic profiling (Tempus xT panel)

Comment: Given the patient's clinical history (never smoker) and Stage IIIB presentation, comprehensive molecular testing including EGFR, ALK, ROS1, BRAF, MET, RET, HER2, KRAS, and PD-L1 assessment has been ordered.

COMPREHENSIVE GENOMIC PROFILING REPORT

Laboratory: Tempus Labs, Inc.
Test: Tempus xT (648-gene panel, DNA and RNA)

SPECIMEN INFORMATION:
Specimen Type: Formalin-fixed paraffin-embedded (FFPE) tissue
Specimen Site: Left lower lobe lung, CT-guided core biopsy
Specimen ID: SP-25-04821
Tumor Purity: 55%
Sequencing Coverage: Mean depth 750x
Specimen Adequacy: Adequate for testing

DIAGNOSIS: Non-small cell lung cancer, adenocarcinoma

GENOMIC FINDINGS:

Clinically Relevant Alterations Identified:

1. EGFR exon 19 deletion (p.L747_T751del, c.2239_2253del15)
   Variant Allele Frequency (VAF): 44%
   Tier: I - Strong clinical significance
   Interpretation: This in-frame deletion in exon 19 of EGFR is a well-characterized activating mutation associated with sensitivity to EGFR tyrosine kinase inhibitors (TKIs) including osimertinib, erlotinib, afatinib, and gefitinib. EGFR exon 19 deletions are the most common EGFR activating mutations in NSCLC, accounting for approximately 45% of EGFR-mutant cases. Current NCCN guidelines recommend osimertinib as preferred first-line therapy for EGFR-mutant NSCLC.

2. TP53 p.G266V (c.797G>T, exon 8)
   Variant Allele Frequency (VAF): 38%
   Tier: II - Potential clinical significance
   Interpretation: Missense mutation in the DNA-binding domain of TP53. This variant is classified as pathogenic. TP53 mutations co-occur frequently with EGFR mutations in lung adenocarcinoma and may be associated with shorter progression-free survival on EGFR TKI therapy in some series.

3. APC p.R876* (c.2626C>T, exon 16)
   Variant Allele Frequency (VAF): 15%
   Tier: III - Variant of unknown clinical significance in this context
   Interpretation: Truncating nonsense mutation in APC. While APC loss-of-function mutations are pathogenic drivers in colorectal cancer, the clinical significance in NSCLC is uncertain. This may represent a subclonal passenger event.

BIOMARKER RESULTS:
  PD-L1 (22C3 pharmDx): Tumor Proportion Score (TPS) = 5% (low expression)
  Tumor Mutational Burden (TMB): 3 mutations/Mb (low)
  Microsatellite Status: Microsatellite Stable (MSS)

NEGATIVE FINDINGS (no pathogenic alterations detected):
  ALK rearrangement: Not detected (by DNA and RNA)
  ROS1 rearrangement: Not detected (by DNA and RNA)
  BRAF V600E or other BRAF mutations: Not detected
  MET exon 14 skipping: Not detected
  MET amplification: Not detected
  RET rearrangement: Not detected
  HER2 (ERBB2) mutations or amplification: Not detected
  KRAS mutations: Not detected
  NTRK1/2/3 rearrangements: Not detected
  EGFR T790M: Not detected
  PIK3CA mutations: Not detected
  STK11 mutations: Not detected
  KEAP1 mutations: Not detected

VARIANTS OF UNCERTAIN SIGNIFICANCE:
  DNMT3A p.R474Q (VAF 8%) - Missense variant, uncertain significance
  ARID1A p.P946L (VAF 6%) - Missense variant, uncertain significance

GENOMIC SIGNATURES:
  Homologous Recombination Deficiency: Not detected
  No evidence of EGFR amplification
  No evidence of MYC family amplification

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Newly diagnosed lung cancer, referred for medical oncology evaluation and treatment planning.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
This is a 56-year-old Middle Eastern male, never smoker, who presents for initial medical oncology consultation for a newly diagnosed Stage IIIB non-small cell lung cancer. The patient was found to have an abnormality on a pre-employment chest X-ray approximately 3 weeks ago, which prompted further workup. He reports being previously healthy and entirely asymptomatic. He denies cough, hemoptysis, dyspnea, chest pain, weight loss, or constitutional symptoms. CT imaging revealed a 4.5 cm left lower lobe mass invading the posterolateral chest wall with rib erosion and ipsilateral mediastinal lymphadenopathy at stations 5 and 7. PET/CT confirmed FDG avidity of the primary tumor (SUV max 11.8) and mediastinal nodes (SUV max 9.2) with no distant disease. CT-guided biopsy demonstrated moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma, acinar predominant with focal signet ring features. Immunohistochemistry showed TTF-1+, Napsin A+, CK7+. Comprehensive genomic profiling via Tempus xT panel returned showing an EGFR exon 19 deletion (p.L747_T751del) at 44% VAF, with co-occurring TP53 G266V and APC truncating mutations. PD-L1 TPS was 5% and TMB was 3 mutations/Mb. Notably, no ALK, ROS1, BRAF, KRAS, MET, RET, or HER2 alterations were identified. A staging brain MRI has been ordered and is pending.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
Constitutional: Denies fever, chills, night sweats, or unintentional weight loss. Reports maintaining his usual weight.
Pulmonary: Denies cough, dyspnea, wheezing, or hemoptysis.
Cardiovascular: Denies chest pain, palpitations, or edema.
Gastrointestinal: Denies nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, or abdominal pain. Appetite is good.
Musculoskeletal: Denies back pain or bone pain. No left-sided chest wall discomfort.
Neurological: Denies headaches, vision changes, weakness, numbness, or seizures.
Skin: No rashes.

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:
No significant past medical history. No prior surgeries. No chronic medications.

SOCIAL HISTORY:
Never smoker. No alcohol use. No recreational drug use. Works as a civil engineer. Married with two adult children. Born in Lebanon, immigrated 20 years ago. Lives with his wife.

FAMILY HISTORY:
Father died of myocardial infarction at age 72. Mother alive at age 80, history of type 2 diabetes. One brother, healthy. No family history of cancer.

ALLERGIES:
No known drug allergies.

MEDICATIONS:
None.

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
Vitals: BP 128/78, HR 72, RR 16, Temp 98.4 F, SpO2 98% on room air, Weight 82 kg, Height 175 cm, BMI 26.8.
General: Well-appearing male in no acute distress. ECOG performance status 0.
HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Oropharynx clear. No cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy.
Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, rales, or rhonchi. No dullness to percussion.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. Normal S1, S2. No murmurs, gallops, or rubs.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No hepatomegaly or palpable masses.
Extremities: No cyanosis, clubbing, or edema.
Neurological: Alert and oriented. Cranial nerves II-XII intact. Motor strength 5/5 throughout.
Skin: No suspicious lesions or rashes.

LAB RESULTS:
CBC: WBC 7.1 x10^9/L, Hgb 14.8 g/dL, Plt 268 x10^9/L
CMP: Na 140, K 4.2, Cl 102, CO2 24, BUN 14, Cr 0.9 mg/dL, Glucose 98, Albumin 4.1, Total Protein 7.2, ALT 22 U/L, AST 19 U/L, Alk Phos 72, Total Bilirubin 0.6
CEA: 4.8 ng/mL (slightly elevated)
LDH: 195 U/L

IMAGING RESULTS:
CT Chest/Abdomen/Pelvis: 4.5 cm left lower lobe mass with chest wall invasion and rib erosion. Mediastinal lymphadenopathy stations 5 and 7 (largest 2.3 cm). No pleural effusion. No hepatic, adrenal, or osseous metastases.
PET/CT: FDG-avid LLL mass SUV max 11.8, mediastinal nodes SUV max 9.2. No distant FDG-avid disease.
MRI Brain: Pending.

ASSESSMENT:
56-year-old never-smoker male with Stage IIIB (T3N2M0) EGFR exon 19 deletion-positive left lower lobe adenocarcinoma of the lung with chest wall invasion and ipsilateral mediastinal lymphadenopathy. The disease is unresectable given the extent of chest wall involvement and N2 nodal disease. Molecular profiling confirms EGFR exon 19 deletion, which is the most favorable EGFR mutation subtype for TKI sensitivity.

PLAN:
1. Staging brain MRI ordered to complete staging workup - results pending at time of this visit.
2. This case was discussed at multidisciplinary tumor board. Consensus recommendation is definitive concurrent chemoradiation followed by consolidation osimertinib, based on the LAURA trial data demonstrating significant PFS benefit for osimertinib consolidation after chemoradiation in unresectable EGFR-mutant Stage III NSCLC.
3. Chemoradiation regimen: Carboplatin AUC 2 weekly x 6 weeks concurrent with thoracic radiation 60 Gy in 30 fractions.
4. Radiation oncology consultation has been arranged.
5. After completion of chemoradiation and recovery from acute toxicities, plan to initiate consolidation osimertinib 80 mg daily.
6. Pre-treatment labs and pulmonary function testing to be obtained.
7. Discussed diagnosis, prognosis, and treatment plan with patient and his wife at length. Patient understands the goals of treatment and wishes to proceed. He was counseled regarding potential side effects of chemoradiation including fatigue, esophagitis, pneumonitis, and myelosuppression.
8. Return in 1 week for radiation oncology simulation and treatment initiation.

RADIOLOGY REPORT

STUDY TYPE: CT Chest/Abdomen/Pelvis with IV Contrast

CLINICAL INDICATION: Newly diagnosed left lower lobe lung mass. Staging.

TECHNIQUE: Helical CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed following administration of 100 mL of Omnipaque 350 intravenous contrast. Axial images were acquired at 1.25 mm slice thickness with coronal and sagittal reformats.

FINDINGS:

Chest:
Lungs: There is a 4.5 x 3.8 x 4.1 cm spiculated mass centered in the left lower lobe, posterior basal segment. The mass abuts and invades the posterolateral chest wall with erosion of the adjacent 9th rib. There is associated soft tissue thickening along the inner chest wall. No endobronchial lesion is identified. The left lower lobe bronchus is patent. No satellite nodules are seen in the left lung. The right lung is clear without nodules or masses. No pleural effusion bilaterally. No pneumothorax.

Mediastinum: There is pathologically enlarged lymphadenopathy at station 5 (aortopulmonary window) measuring 1.8 cm in short axis and station 7 (subcarinal) measuring 2.3 cm in short axis. No enlarged hilar, paratracheal, or prevascular lymph nodes. Heart is normal in size. No pericardial effusion. Thoracic aorta and great vessels are normal.

Abdomen:
Liver: Normal in size and attenuation. No focal hepatic lesions.
Gallbladder and biliary tree: Unremarkable.
Pancreas: Normal.
Spleen: Normal in size.
Adrenal glands: Normal bilaterally. No adrenal masses or nodules.
Kidneys: Normal bilaterally. No hydronephrosis or renal masses.
Retroperitoneum: No retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy.

Pelvis:
Bladder: Normal. Prostate appears normal for age.
No pelvic lymphadenopathy. No free fluid.

Bones: Erosion of the left 9th rib posteriorly at the site of tumor contact, as described. No other suspicious osseous lesions. Degenerative changes of the lumbar spine.

IMPRESSION:
1. 4.5 cm left lower lobe spiculated mass with invasion of the posterolateral chest wall and erosion of the left 9th rib, consistent with the known primary lung malignancy (T3).
2. Pathologically enlarged mediastinal lymphadenopathy at stations 5 (1.8 cm) and 7 (2.3 cm), suspicious for metastatic nodal involvement (N2).
3. No evidence of distant metastatic disease in the abdomen or pelvis (M0).
4. Overall staging assessment: T3N2M0, Stage IIIB.

RADIOLOGY REPORT

STUDY TYPE: PET/CT (18F-FDG)

CLINICAL INDICATION: Left lower lobe lung adenocarcinoma staging. Evaluate for distant metastatic disease.

TECHNIQUE: Following a 6-hour fast, the patient's blood glucose was 94 mg/dL. 12.4 mCi of 18F-FDG was administered intravenously. After a 60-minute uptake period, PET/CT images were acquired from skull base to mid-thigh with low-dose CT for attenuation correction and anatomic correlation.

FINDINGS:

Head and Neck: Physiologic uptake in the brain. No abnormal cervical or supraclavicular lymph node uptake.

Chest:
Left Lower Lobe Mass: Intensely FDG-avid spiculated mass in the left lower lobe measuring 4.5 cm with SUV max of 11.8. The mass demonstrates chest wall invasion as previously noted on diagnostic CT.

Mediastinal Lymph Nodes: FDG-avid lymphadenopathy at station 5 (aortopulmonary window) with SUV max 7.4 and station 7 (subcarinal) with SUV max 9.2. No FDG-avid hilar, paratracheal, or contralateral mediastinal lymphadenopathy.

Lungs: No additional FDG-avid pulmonary nodules. No pleural uptake.

Abdomen and Pelvis:
Liver: Homogeneous physiologic uptake. No focal hypermetabolic hepatic lesions.
Adrenal Glands: No abnormal adrenal uptake bilaterally.
Kidneys: Normal physiologic excretion.
Bowel: Physiologic bowel activity. No suspicious focal uptake.
No FDG-avid retroperitoneal or pelvic lymphadenopathy.

Musculoskeletal: No FDG-avid osseous lesions. Physiologic marrow activity throughout the axial and appendicular skeleton.

IMPRESSION:
1. Intensely FDG-avid left lower lobe mass (SUV max 11.8) consistent with primary lung malignancy.
2. FDG-avid ipsilateral mediastinal lymphadenopathy at stations 5 (SUV max 7.4) and 7 (SUV max 9.2), consistent with metastatic nodal involvement.
3. No evidence of distant FDG-avid metastatic disease.
4. Findings consistent with Stage IIIB (T3N2M0) disease.

RADIOLOGY REPORT

STUDY TYPE: MRI Brain with and without IV Contrast

CLINICAL INDICATION: Newly diagnosed Stage IIIB NSCLC, adenocarcinoma with EGFR exon 19 deletion. Staging to evaluate for intracranial metastases.

TECHNIQUE: Multiplanar, multisequence MRI of the brain was performed on a 3T scanner before and after intravenous administration of 15 mL of gadolinium-based contrast (gadobutrol). Sequences include axial T1, T2, FLAIR, diffusion-weighted imaging, susceptibility-weighted imaging, and post-contrast T1 in axial, coronal, and sagittal planes.

FINDINGS:

Brain Parenchyma: No enhancing lesions are identified on post-contrast imaging. No restricted diffusion to suggest acute infarct. No mass effect or midline shift. The gray-white matter differentiation is preserved throughout. No signal abnormality in the cerebral hemispheres, basal ganglia, thalami, brainstem, or cerebellum.

Ventricles and Cisterns: The ventricular system is normal in size and configuration. No hydrocephalus. The basal cisterns are patent.

Extra-axial Spaces: No extra-axial fluid collections. No dural enhancement. The calvarium and skull base are unremarkable.

Orbits and Paranasal Sinuses: Unremarkable. Mild mucosal thickening in the left maxillary sinus, likely incidental.

IMPRESSION:
1. No evidence of intracranial metastatic disease.
2. Normal MRI of the brain.
3. Incidental mild left maxillary sinus mucosal thickening.

RADIOLOGY REPORT

STUDY TYPE: CT Chest/Abdomen/Pelvis with IV Contrast

CLINICAL INDICATION: Stage IIIB NSCLC, EGFR exon 19 deletion positive. Post-concurrent chemoradiation restaging. Evaluate treatment response.

TECHNIQUE: Helical CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed following administration of 100 mL of Omnipaque 350 intravenous contrast. Axial images were acquired at 1.25 mm slice thickness with coronal and sagittal reformats.

Comparison: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis and PET/CT from approximately 8 weeks prior.

FINDINGS:

Chest:
Lungs: The previously identified left lower lobe mass has decreased in size, now measuring 2.2 x 1.9 x 2.0 cm (previously 4.5 x 3.8 x 4.1 cm), representing a 51% decrease in longest diameter. The mass remains in the posterior basal segment. The previously noted chest wall invasion appears improved with interval decrease in the soft tissue component along the posterolateral chest wall. The 9th rib erosion is unchanged. There are new patchy ground-glass opacities in the left lower lobe surrounding the primary site and extending into the lingula, consistent with radiation pneumonitis in the expected radiation field. Mild linear atelectasis at the left base. Right lung is clear.

Mediastinum: Previously enlarged lymph nodes at station 5 and station 7 have resolved. Station 5 node now measures 0.8 cm (previously 1.8 cm). Station 7 node now measures 0.9 cm (previously 2.3 cm). No new lymphadenopathy.

Abdomen:
Liver: Normal. No hepatic lesions.
Adrenal glands: Normal bilaterally.
Kidneys: Normal.
No retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy.

Pelvis: Unremarkable.

Bones: Stable left 9th rib erosion. No new osseous lesions.

IMPRESSION:
1. Significant partial response to chemoradiation with decrease in the left lower lobe mass from 4.5 cm to 2.2 cm (51% decrease in longest diameter). Per RECIST 1.1 criteria, this represents a partial response.
2. Resolution of previously enlarged mediastinal lymphadenopathy at stations 5 and 7.
3. New ground-glass opacities in the left lower lobe and lingula consistent with radiation pneumonitis.
4. No evidence of distant metastatic disease.

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Follow-up after concurrent chemoradiation for Stage IIIB NSCLC.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
This is a 56-year-old Middle Eastern male with Stage IIIB (T3N2M0) EGFR exon 19 deletion-positive left lower lobe adenocarcinoma who completed concurrent chemoradiation approximately 6 weeks ago. He received carboplatin AUC 2 weekly for 6 cycles concurrent with 60 Gy in 30 fractions of thoracic radiation. Four weeks after completing radiation, he developed increasing dyspnea on exertion and dry cough. CT imaging demonstrated patchy ground-glass opacities consistent with grade 2 radiation pneumonitis. He was started on prednisone 40 mg daily with planned slow taper over 8 weeks. He reports that his dyspnea has improved significantly since starting steroids. He is currently on prednisone 20 mg daily (week 4 of taper). He has mild difficulty sleeping attributed to the steroids. No fevers. Appetite is fair. He has lost about 3 kg during treatment which he is slowly regaining.

Restaging CT from last week shows excellent partial response with the primary mass decreased from 4.5 to 2.2 cm and resolution of mediastinal lymphadenopathy. Ground-glass opacities consistent with radiation pneumonitis are present but he is clinically improving.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
Constitutional: Mild fatigue, improving. Weight stable this week.
Pulmonary: Dyspnea on exertion improved from prior. Mild dry cough. No hemoptysis.
Gastrointestinal: Mild increased appetite on steroids. No nausea or diarrhea.
Musculoskeletal: Mild proximal muscle weakness attributed to steroids.
Psychiatric: Difficulty sleeping on steroids, using melatonin with some benefit.
Skin: No rash. No acne from prednisone.
All other systems reviewed and negative.

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
Vitals: BP 134/82, HR 78, RR 18, Temp 98.6 F, SpO2 96% on room air, Weight 79 kg.
General: Well-appearing male. Mild Cushingoid facies. ECOG PS 1.
Lungs: Faint crackles at the left base. No wheezing. Right lung clear.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender. No hepatomegaly.
Extremities: No edema. Trace proximal lower extremity weakness bilaterally (4+/5).

LAB RESULTS:
CBC: WBC 9.8 x10^9/L (leukocytosis, steroid effect), Hgb 13.2 g/dL, Plt 245 x10^9/L
CMP: Na 141, K 3.8, Cr 0.8, ALT 18, AST 20, Albumin 3.8
CEA: 3.2 ng/mL (decreased from 4.8 at diagnosis)
Glucose: 118 mg/dL (steroid effect)

IMAGING RESULTS:
CT Chest/Abdomen/Pelvis: Left lower lobe mass decreased from 4.5 to 2.2 cm (partial response per RECIST 1.1). Mediastinal lymphadenopathy resolved. Ground-glass opacities in left lower lobe consistent with radiation pneumonitis. No distant disease.

ASSESSMENT:
56-year-old male with Stage IIIB EGFR exon 19 deletion-positive NSCLC with excellent partial response to concurrent chemoradiation. Currently being managed for grade 2 radiation pneumonitis which is improving on prednisone taper. CEA trending down from 4.8 to 3.2.

PLAN:
1. Continue prednisone taper: reduce to 15 mg daily next week, then 10 mg x 1 week, then 5 mg x 1 week, then discontinue. Total taper duration approximately 8 weeks.
2. Once prednisone is below 10 mg daily and pneumonitis is stable or improved, plan to initiate consolidation osimertinib (Tagrisso) 80 mg daily per the LAURA trial protocol.
3. Will obtain baseline ECG and echocardiogram prior to starting osimertinib given known QTc prolongation risk.
4. Monitor for pneumonitis flare during steroid taper and after osimertinib initiation, as osimertinib carries its own pneumonitis risk.
5. Continue supportive care with melatonin for insomnia.
6. Return in 3 weeks for reassessment and potential osimertinib initiation.
7. Patient counseled on osimertinib side effects including diarrhea, rash, paronychia, and pneumonitis risk. He understands and agrees with plan.

RADIOLOGY REPORT

STUDY TYPE: CT Chest/Abdomen/Pelvis with IV Contrast

CLINICAL INDICATION: Stage IIIB NSCLC, EGFR exon 19 deletion positive. On consolidation osimertinib x 3 months. Restaging.

TECHNIQUE: Helical CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with IV contrast per standard protocol.

Comparison: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis from approximately 3 months prior.

FINDINGS:

Chest:
Lungs: The left lower lobe mass has further decreased in size, now measuring 1.8 x 1.5 cm (previously 2.2 x 1.9 cm). There are evolving fibrotic changes at the site of the primary lesion with parenchymal bands and volume loss in the left lower lobe. The previously noted ground-glass opacities consistent with radiation pneumonitis have resolved. Residual linear scarring in the left lower lobe and lingula. Right lung remains clear. No new pulmonary nodules.

Mediastinum: No mediastinal lymphadenopathy. Station 5 and 7 nodes remain subcentimeter.

Abdomen: No hepatic lesions. Adrenal glands normal. Kidneys normal. No retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy.

Pelvis: Unremarkable.

Bones: Stable chronic changes in the left 9th rib. Healing erosion. No new osseous lesions.

IMPRESSION:
1. Continued response to consolidation osimertinib with further decrease in the left lower lobe mass from 2.2 cm to 1.8 cm.
2. Resolution of radiation pneumonitis with residual fibrotic changes.
3. No evidence of recurrence or distant metastatic disease.

RADIOLOGY REPORT

STUDY TYPE: CT Chest/Abdomen/Pelvis with IV Contrast

CLINICAL INDICATION: Stage IIIB NSCLC on consolidation osimertinib. 6-month surveillance.

TECHNIQUE: Helical CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with IV contrast per standard protocol.

Comparison: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis from approximately 3 months prior.

FINDINGS:

Chest:
Lungs: Left lower lobe residual mass/fibrosis measures 1.6 x 1.3 cm (previously 1.8 x 1.5 cm), minimally decreased and likely predominantly fibrotic. Post-radiation fibrotic changes are stable with parenchymal bands and mild volume loss in the left lower lobe. No new pulmonary nodules. Right lung clear.

Mediastinum: No lymphadenopathy.

Abdomen: Liver, spleen, pancreas, adrenal glands, and kidneys are unremarkable. No new findings.

Pelvis: Unremarkable.

Bones: Stable healing of left 9th rib. No new osseous lesions.

IMPRESSION:
1. Stable to minimally decreased left lower lobe residual mass/fibrosis, now 1.6 cm. Findings likely represent post-treatment fibrosis.
2. Stable post-radiation fibrotic changes.
3. No evidence of disease recurrence or distant metastatic disease.

RADIOLOGY REPORT

STUDY TYPE: CT Chest/Abdomen/Pelvis with IV Contrast

CLINICAL INDICATION: Stage IIIB NSCLC, EGFR mutant, on consolidation osimertinib. 9-month surveillance.

TECHNIQUE: Helical CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with IV contrast per standard protocol.

Comparison: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis from approximately 3 months prior.

FINDINGS:

Chest:
Lungs: Left lower lobe residual fibrotic mass measures 1.5 x 1.2 cm, essentially stable compared to prior (previously 1.6 x 1.3 cm). Post-radiation fibrosis stable. No new nodules or masses. Right lung clear.

Mediastinum: No lymphadenopathy. Stable post-treatment changes.

Abdomen: Unremarkable. No hepatic lesions, adrenal abnormalities, or lymphadenopathy.

Pelvis: Unremarkable.

Bones: Healed left 9th rib. No new osseous lesions.

IMPRESSION:
1. Stable left lower lobe residual fibrotic mass measuring 1.5 cm. No evidence of progression.
2. Stable post-radiation fibrosis.
3. No recurrence or distant metastatic disease.

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Routine surveillance visit, on consolidation osimertinib for 9 months.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
This is a 56-year-old Middle Eastern male with Stage IIIB (T3N2M0) EGFR exon 19 deletion-positive left lower lobe adenocarcinoma who completed definitive concurrent chemoradiation and has now been on consolidation osimertinib (Tagrisso) 80 mg daily for 9 months. He is feeling well overall. He reports no respiratory symptoms - no cough, dyspnea, or chest pain. His energy level is back to baseline and he has returned to work full-time. His weight has recovered to near his pre-treatment baseline. He reports mild intermittent loose stools (1-2 episodes per week) which he attributes to the osimertinib but finds manageable. He has noticed mild dry skin and occasional periungual tenderness of his thumbnails but no paronychia requiring intervention. He denies any rash. No mouth sores. No visual changes.

The episode of grade 1 pneumonitis-like symptoms that occurred shortly after starting osimertinib resolved completely with a brief steroid course and has not recurred. He has had no further respiratory issues.

Surveillance CT from this week shows stable left lower lobe residual fibrotic mass at 1.5 cm, unchanged from 3 months ago. No new lesions and no distant disease.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
Constitutional: Feels well. No weight loss, fevers, or night sweats.
Pulmonary: No cough, dyspnea, or hemoptysis.
Cardiovascular: No chest pain or palpitations.
Gastrointestinal: Mild intermittent loose stools 1-2x/week. No nausea.
Dermatologic: Mild dry skin. Periungual tenderness of thumbnails.
Musculoskeletal: No pain.
Neurological: No headaches, vision changes, or neurologic symptoms.

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
Vitals: BP 124/76, HR 70, RR 16, Temp 98.2 F, SpO2 98% on room air, Weight 81 kg.
General: Well-appearing, well-nourished male. No distress. ECOG PS 0.
Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No crackles.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. Normal S1/S2.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender.
Skin: Mild diffuse xerosis. Periungual erythema and mild tenderness of bilateral thumbnails without frank paronychia or infection.
Extremities: No edema.

LAB RESULTS:
CBC: WBC 6.4 x10^9/L, Hgb 14.5 g/dL, Plt 258 x10^9/L
CMP: Na 139, K 4.3, Cr 0.9, ALT 28 U/L, AST 24 U/L, Albumin 4.2
CEA: 2.1 ng/mL (within normal limits, down from 3.2)

IMAGING RESULTS:
CT Chest/Abdomen/Pelvis: Stable residual fibrotic mass in left lower lobe at 1.5 cm. No evidence of progression. No new lesions. No distant disease.

ASSESSMENT:
56-year-old male with Stage IIIB EGFR exon 19 deletion-positive NSCLC, status post concurrent chemoradiation with excellent partial response, now 9 months into consolidation osimertinib with continued stable disease. CEA has normalized at 2.1. He is tolerating treatment well with only mild GI and dermatologic side effects. ECOG PS has returned to 0.

PLAN:
1. Continue osimertinib 80 mg daily. Per the LAURA trial, consolidation osimertinib is continued until disease progression or unacceptable toxicity.
2. Surveillance CT chest/abdomen/pelvis in 3 months.
3. Surveillance brain MRI to be scheduled in 6 months given EGFR-mutant histology and known CNS tropism.
4. For mild diarrhea: continue PRN loperamide. Dietary counseling provided.
5. For dry skin: recommended gentle moisturizer and fragrance-free products. If periungual symptoms worsen, will consider biotin supplementation or referral to dermatology.
6. ECG at next visit to monitor QTc interval.
7. Return in 3 months with labs and imaging.

RADIOLOGY REPORT

STUDY TYPE: CT Chest/Abdomen/Pelvis with IV Contrast

CLINICAL INDICATION: Stage IIIB NSCLC, EGFR mutant, on consolidation osimertinib. 12-month surveillance.

TECHNIQUE: Helical CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with IV contrast per standard protocol.

Comparison: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis from approximately 3 months prior.

FINDINGS:

Chest:
Lungs: Left lower lobe residual fibrotic mass measures 1.5 x 1.2 cm, stable compared to prior. Post-radiation fibrosis with parenchymal bands and volume loss in the left lower lobe is stable. No new pulmonary nodules or masses. No pleural effusion. Right lung clear.

Mediastinum: No lymphadenopathy. Heart size normal. No pericardial effusion.

Abdomen: Liver is normal in size and attenuation without focal lesions. Gallbladder, pancreas, and spleen are unremarkable. Adrenal glands are normal bilaterally. Kidneys are normal. No retroperitoneal or mesenteric lymphadenopathy. No ascites.

Pelvis: Unremarkable. No pelvic lymphadenopathy.

Bones: Healed left 9th rib changes, stable. No new suspicious osseous lesions.

IMPRESSION:
1. Stable left lower lobe residual fibrotic mass measuring 1.5 cm, unchanged from prior examinations. Represents post-treatment fibrosis without evidence of active disease.
2. Stable post-radiation changes.
3. No evidence of local recurrence or distant metastatic disease.
4. Continued stable disease on consolidation osimertinib at 12 months.
